headline: Chip Surge Meets War Fatigue: Markets Brace for Another Volatile Open
body_html:
Markets head into Thursday's open carrying the weight of Wednesday's broad retreat, after equities sold off sharply on President Trump's declaration that any prospect of an Iran peace agreement is finished. Breadth told the story plainly: only 122 names in our 396-stock universe closed higher, against 272 decliners. Homebuilders bore the worst of it, shedding 4.24% as a sector, while Healthcare fell 1.69% and Consumer names dropped 2.21%. The defensive rotation was unmistakable.

The geopolitical backdrop remains the dominant variable heading into the session. Fresh U.S. strikes against Iran pushed crude sharply higher overnight before oil prices surrendered some gains as traders weighed the longer-term demand implications. Gold climbed from a one-week low, aided by a softer dollar in early Asian hours — though the greenback has shown renewed strength on Gulf-conflict safe-haven flows and rising Federal Reserve rate-hike speculation. The Bank of Japan flagged mounting inflation pressures tied directly to the conflict.

Against that backdrop, semiconductor names offer a rare pocket of resilience. The sector gained 2.41% on Wednesday, the only major group to finish in the green, with networking and power-chip names leading the advance. Chip momentum will be a key watch at the open, as will any further headline risk out of the Middle East before the first trade prints.

briefs.headline: Trump Kills Iran Deal Hope, Sending Stocks Lower Wednesday
briefs.body: Equities closed Wednesday in the red after President Trump declared the Iran peace process dead, triggering a broad risk-off move. The S&P 500 ended the session lower as investors recalibrated for a prolonged conflict scenario. The hawkish tone raised fresh questions about the trajectory of oil supply and Federal Reserve policy.

briefs.headline: Semiconductors Defy the Selloff, Gaining 2.41% as a Group
briefs.body: While most sectors retreated sharply on Wednesday, chip-related names staged a notable divergence, with the semiconductor cohort advancing 2.41%. Networking equipment maker Arista Networks surged 8.76% and power-semiconductor specialist Alpha and Omega climbed 7.48%, reflecting sustained investor appetite for infrastructure tied to AI and data-center buildout.

briefs.headline: Akamai Rockets 10.67%, Leads the Day's Gainers
briefs.body: Internet-infrastructure firm Akamai posted the session's sharpest advance, closing up 10.67% on Wednesday. The move lifted the broader internet-infrastructure space and drew attention to companies positioned along the digital backbone as enterprises accelerate capacity investment. No single catalyst was confirmed, but the move was decisive on elevated volume.

briefs.headline: Homebuilders Hammered as Rate and Conflict Fears Combine
briefs.body: The homebuilder sector was Wednesday's worst performer, sliding 4.24% as rising oil prices stoked inflation fears and Fed rate-hike speculation intensified. Higher-for-longer rate expectations weigh directly on mortgage affordability, pressuring new-home demand assumptions. Carlisle Companies, a building-products supplier, fell 6.46%, adding to the sector's pain.

briefs.headline: LNG Prices Surge as Iran War Lifts Liquefaction Revenues
briefs.body: Energy markets are registering the Iran conflict's reshaping of global gas flows. Venture Global reported that its second-quarter liquefaction fees jumped 69% year-over-year, a figure that underscores how the war has tightened LNG supply chains. The Energy sector was Wednesday's second-best performer, advancing 1.09%.

briefs.headline: U.S.-Spain Trade Halt Adds Another Front to Market Uncertainty
briefs.body: President Trump ordered a suspension of U.S. trade with Spain, citing disputes over NATO spending commitments and Madrid's stance on Iran, adding a fresh geopolitical layer for markets already navigating Gulf-conflict risk. The move threatens to complicate transatlantic supply chains and may weigh on multinational Consumer and Industrial names at the open.
